items.fix: Profile first, optimize second. Confirm the bottleneck is what you think.
items.why: Most performance intuition is wrong; the slow function is rarely where it 'looks' slow.
items.example: Team spent two days rewriting a tokenizer; cProfile showed the slow part was `json.loads` elsewhere.

items.mistake: Sorting only by `cumulative` and stopping there.
items.fix: Sort by `tottime` next — that's where the actual CPU work is.
items.why: High cumtime / low tottime functions are just routers; the real fix is inside their children.

items.mistake: Profiling a too-small input (`N=10`) and assuming the result holds at scale.
items.fix: Profile with a realistic workload — at least the smallest input that triggers the bottleneck.
items.why: At tiny N, constant factors dominate; the asymptotic culprit hides until N grows.
items.example: Profile with N=10,000 docs (or whatever production-like) — not with N=3.

items.mistake: Comparing optimisations on different machines / cold caches.
items.fix: Run before and after on the same machine, the same warm caches, the same input.
items.why: Performance numbers are only meaningful in apples-to-apples comparison.
items.example: Warm up first (`run_pipeline()` once), then profile; repeat 3× and take the median.

items.fix: Use cProfile in dev/staging; for production sampling use `py-spy --duration N`.
items.why: cProfile's 2-5× slowdown is fine in dev but unacceptable for user-facing traffic.
items.example: `py-spy record --pid <pid> --duration 30 -o flamegraph.svg` for live prod sampling.
